text: Hi, I'm Vahag Nicolian and today we'll be reviewing a pretty straightforward robotic tapping renal hernia repair in a patient who had a recurrence after anterior mesh repair.
This is a fifty-two year old man who presented with no major medical problems and a recurrent hernia. He had an open renal hernia and a prior open umbilical hernia repair.
We did not have operative reports, but he knew that mesh had been used. He presented with a reducible inguinal hernia on exam.
We moved forward with the operation, robotic repair.

The video shows a classic medial recurrence of an inguinal hernia after a mesh-based repair, likely a Lichtenstein repair, as the surgeon prepares to perform a standard robotic tap inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap on the right side, starting laterally and moving medially, passing through the umbilical ligaments and proceeding with a general dissection towards the midline.
Because this patient has a medial hernia, I do not try to identify pupuses. That will be challenging. And I want to really focus in on isolating this hernia before attacking it.

The surgeon performs a lateral dissection in the visceral compartment, developing the preperitoneal flap. The intermediate fascia is visible, and tissue is reflected up against the sidewall to minimize neuropathic pain.
The surgeon performs a limited lateral dissection, temporarily pausing the thorough dissection for later in the procedure.
The surgeon focuses on dissecting the intermediate fascia, working within the visceral compartment on both medial and lateral aspects, while looking back towards the midline.
The surgeon transitions to the parietal component of the dissection as they approach the pubic region, continuing the surgical procedure.
The surgeon identifies the incarcerated preperitoneal fat and locates the pseudosac. The hernia is then reduced as the pseudosac is clearly visible.
The surgeon focuses on the retrocubic dissection, aggressively developing the space beyond two centimeters between Cooper's ligament and the bladder.
The surgeon identifies the levator muscles and the obturator internus muscle near the midline, focusing on the anatomical structures visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon extends the dissection two centimeters beyond the midline to identify the contralateral Cooper's ligament, preparing it as a fixation point for mesh placement in the medial hernia repair.
The surgeon identifies the contralateral Cooper's ligament and performs a medial dissection, developing the surgical plane laterally.
The surgeon reflects the spermatic vessels to the side wall and parietalizes the vas deferens, creating an adequate pocket. The iliopsoas muscle is identified during this process.

The surgeon examines the anatomy, identifying the lacunar ligament and the medial hernia, while observing the indirect space and the iliopubic tract. The surgeon rules out a lipoma and assesses the parietalization and retropubic dissection, preparing to secure the mid-weight mesh at the ipsilateral and contralateral Cooper's ligaments.
In terms of my knots, I like to Leave them as air knots unless I encounter bleeding with the stitch. I find that when you tighten these knots, it can deform the mesh. So avoid that.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap from lateral to medial, starting with the lateral aspect under tension and finishing with the redundant medial peritoneum.
Very standard repair here. I do not regularly evacuate the air from the preperitoneal space. Never did so in fellowship and have not changed that practice. And there we are.
The surgeon addresses a small fenestration in the surgical field.
